Proposition 4 (Jensen's Inequality). Let be a random variable with , and let be a convex1 function. Then

If is concave, the inequality is reversed. The equality is saturated when is linear

Proof

Take a linear approximation at . By convexity this line lies below . Thus

(9)

□

Informally, Jensen’s inequality says that convex transformations pull expectations upwards. For example, is convex, so the variance is always positive.

Corollary. For a convex loss function, averaging predictions never increases the expected loss.

1.4. Martingales

[TODO] Review gloria mathcamp handout and “bold play” problem.

A sequence of fair games shouldn’t lead to an unfair outcome. Let’s try to formalize this.

Definition 2. A discrete stochastic process is a martingale w.r.t. a filtration if

  1. is -measurable. That is, the value of can be determined given everything you know up to time inclusive.
  3. .

A submartingale/supermartingale replaces condition 3 with and , respectively. Note, “sub” means “increasing” here!

Note. The one-step martingale property (#3), automatically implies for fixed deterministic times , by applying the tower law repeatedly.

Examples:

  1. Random walk: , where are i.i.d. with mean , and .

  2. Cumulative coin flips: ,

    (20)

  3. Squared random walk is strictly a submartingale:
  4. Let be an indicator variable for whether you win the game. Define as . Then is a martingale by the tower law.

Proposition 6. Let be the position of random walk. Then is also a martingale. More generally, let . Then the following is also a martingale:

(21)

This allows us to compute the variance of or the expectation of the stopping time if we know the other (See after Example 5).

1.4.1. Stopping Times

Definition 3. A random time is a stopping time (wrt ) if the event can be decided using only info up to time .

Examples include: first time you hit in a random walk or leave the interval .

Theorem 3 (Optional Stopping Theorem). Let be a martingale, a stopping time. Under mild conditions,

(22)

One of the three following conditions must hold

  1. is bounded a.s.
  2. and increments are bounded.
  3. is uniformly integrable, i.e. the martingale cannot accumulate arbitrarily large expectation from tails as grows.
Proof

The OST is indeed conveying something nontrivial. Unlike the tower law statement above, need not be deterministic.

  • For bounded stopping times it’s just the tower law.
  • Unbounded stopping times with finite expectation (e.g. ), which is unbounded but has finite expectation. Proof requires dominated convergence.

□

Informally, the OST says that you can’t do anything at the beginning of a fair game to bias your probability of winning / winnings. In the doubling cube game that Alex showed me, if you start with the cube, the dice has value so you’re expected winnings are positive.

An example where OST doesn’t apply: random walk, let be the first time we hit , and let be your position. . The reason OST doesn’t hold is because (check using telescoping catalan sum that and use stirlings formula with third term).

1.4.2. Examples

Example 5 (Random walk with absorption). Suppose we keep playing a random walk until we either win dollars or lose dollars. Since the martingale is bounded from both sides, it is uniformly integratable and the OST applys:

(23)

This holds for general martingales, not just random walks!

As a consistency check that is bounded, consider the martingale , and apply optional stopping. .

Once we’ve proven is bounded, we can use the above results to compute its expectation:

(24)

The proof without martingales would have required solving a recurrence.

Example 6 (Biased random walk). Check that is a martingale where . Then

(25)

The fair case emerges from applying L Hopital.

We can also center the biased random walk to get another martingale: . This is useful for computing expectation of stopping time after we used the other martingale to compute absorption properties.

Some additional facts to derive to boost your confidence:

  • The chance of a ruin with a random walk is .
  • Thus the chance of a biased random walk returning to the origin is . See https://www.desmos.com/calculator/n3x04e116b

1.5. Brownian Motion

Definition 4. A standard Brownian motion is a continuous time stochastic process, where

  2. For , the increment is independent of the past .
  3. (linearly increasing variance).
  4. is almost surely continuous.

Brownian motion is a specific type of Gaussian process with . Intuition: time between and is random noise that doesn’t affect the covariance. Up until time , they have the same noise history. Note that Var(B_s - B_t)= |t-s|.

Definition 5 (Continuous-time martingale). A process is a martingale if

  1. is -measurable.
  2. for all .
  3. For all , .

This is a stronger requirement than the discrete case.

  • By definition, Brownian motion is a martingale.
  • Again by the same reasoning as the discrete case, is also a martingale.
  • is a submartingale by Jensen’s inequality:
  • is a submartingale by Jensen’s inequality.

1.5.1. Bonus: More advanced results

Theorem 4 (Doob's Maximal Inequality). For a submartingale and any positive number ,

(28)

Looks like Markov’s inequality.

Theorem 5 (Arcsine Laws).

  1. The proportion of which a one-dimensional Wiener process is positive follows an arcsine distribution.
  2. The last time the Wiener process changes sign (proportion over ) is arcsine distributed.
  3. The time at which a Wiener process achieves its maximum is arcsin distributed.

The arcsine distribution cdf is:

(29)

The pdf is heavy near 0 and 1 and lowest in the middle. These are all pretty unintuitive considering the pdf.
